title: Cross-Merchant Burst Testing with Escalating Authorization Amounts
narrative: Fraudster conducts rapid-fire authorization testing across 8-12 distinct merchant categories within 45-75 minutes, deliberately varying transaction amounts from $1.47 to $287.50 to map card limits and merchant-specific fraud controls. Pattern shows same card4 hitting gas stations (MCC 5541), digital goods (MCC 5816), electronics retailers (MCC 5732), and travel sites sequentially, with each merchant receiving 1-2 attempts before moving to next target. The attacker uses a consistent device fingerprint (same DeviceInfo hash) but rotates P_emaildomain across free providers (gmail, yahoo, outlook). Card velocity explodes from baseline 2 transactions/week to 11 transactions in 67 minutes, with card1_txn_count_1hr spiking to 11 while card1_txn_count_24hr remains under 15. Fraudster seeks merchants with delayed settlement or weak 3DS enforcement before committing to larger purchases. Final 2-3 transactions jump to $200+ once authorization boundaries are mapped.
indicators: card1_txn_count_1hr >= 8 with 6+ distinct MCC codes represented
TransactionAmt shows stair-step pattern: starts $1-5, mid-sequence $25-75, final attempts $200+
8+ different merchant IDs (card1_merchant_count_1hr >= 8) with 100% new merchant relationships
Consistent DeviceInfo hash across all transactions despite varying P_emaildomain
card1_amt_zscore exceeds 4.5 when comparing 1-hour window to 90-day baseline
Time between transactions averages 4-8 minutes with no gaps exceeding 12 minutes
70%+ of transactions occur within is_night=1 window (22:00-05:00 local merchant time)
summary: The same card hits 8-12 different merchant types in under an hour with amounts climbing from $1.47 to $287.50, while the device fingerprint stays identical but email domains rotate across free providers. Transaction velocity spikes from a normal 2 per week to 11 in 67 minutes, predominantly during nighttime hours.
reasoning: The fraudster is systematically probing stolen card limits and testing which merchants have weak fraud controls before committing to high-value purchases. The escalating amounts, rapid merchant-hopping, and nighttime timing reveal deliberate reconnaissance rather than legitimate shopping behavior.
indicator_explanations: Card rapidly hits multiple unrelated merchant categories in single hour
Transaction amounts climb systematically from pennies to large purchases
Card contacts eight different merchants never used by this cardholder before
Same device is used throughout but email address keeps changing
Hourly spending is drastically higher than this card's normal pattern
Transactions maintain steady drumbeat timing with no natural shopping pauses
Most activity concentrates during overnight hours when monitoring is minimal
